title: Why the Number Should Go Down
body_html:
The title phrase works because it sounds absurd in a culture trained to worship perpetual nominal expansion. But the essay turns that absurdity back on the audience. If entrepreneurs keep discovering how to deliver better goods with fewer inputs, why shouldn’t the number go down? Why should progress be defined as paying the same or more for things that are getting easier to produce?

This is the deepest ideological disagreement underneath the whole piece. One side believes a healthy economy requires managed inflation, permanent stimulus reflexes, and a citizenry kept in motion by fear of cash drag. The other side believes a healthy economy should let productivity gains flow through to households as lower prices, higher purchasing power, and cleaner capital allocation.

Bitcoin sits in the background of the essay as the monetary expression of that second view. Honest money turns falling prices from a policy nightmare into a civilizational payoff. It lets savings store labor across time without silent confiscation and makes state manipulation harder to disguise.

So ‘number go down’ is not merely a contrarian slogan. It is a compressed theory of progress: innovation should make life cheaper, money should tell the truth, and a civilization confident in its productive powers should not need inflation to feel alive.
